== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Sakamizume-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287}} / former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|Hokkeiji}}, {{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}, {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and its former rank was {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{jalink|Maruyama River|Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{jalink|Kyotango|Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{jalink|Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{jalink|Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{jalink|honden|Q2044994}} is a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{jalink|Hakuhō|Q11580612}} 3 ({{jalink|675|Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{jalink|Kokushi Monjo}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{jalink|Koshikoden|Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{jalink|Hokke-ji|Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} 1 ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 7 ({{jalink|1918|Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{jalink|mikoshi|Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{jalink|otabisho|Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{jalink|Eikyō|Q1193059}} 10 ({{jalink|1438|Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1 ({{jalink|1441|Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{jalink|Hōtoku|Q1143606}} 1 ({{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}} 6 ({{jalink|1823|Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{jalink|Ukemochi-no-Mikoto}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{jalink|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
=== Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property (national designation) * Honden (with two munafuda)

=== Related items === * {{jalink|Umenomiya Taisha|Q276781}} - enshrines Sakakeko deity seen in ''Kokushi Monjo''; relation to this shrine as brewing deity has been noted regardless of source authenticity. * {{jalink|Tamaki Matsuri Kitaru Sakake Shrine|Q11612891}} - same context.

== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Sakamizume-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287}} / former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|Hokkeiji}}, {{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}, {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and its former rank was {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{jalink|Maruyama River|Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{jalink|Kyotango|Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{jalink|Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{jalink|Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{jalink|honden|Q2044994}} is a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{jalink|Hakuhō|Q11580612}} 3 ({{jalink|675|Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{jalink|Kokushi Monjo}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{jalink|Koshikoden|Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{jalink|Hokke-ji|Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} 1 ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 7 ({{jalink|1918|Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{jalink|mikoshi|Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{jalink|otabisho|Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{jalink|Eikyō|Q1193059}} 10 ({{jalink|1438|Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1 ({{jalink|1441|Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{jalink|Hōtoku|Q1143606}} 1 ({{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}} 6 ({{jalink|1823|Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{jalink|Ukemochi-no-Mikoto}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{jalink|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>

[[JA:酒垂神社 (豊岡市)]]
{{wikidata link|Q11644305}}
== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Sakamizume-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287}} / former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|Hokkeiji}}, {{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}, {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and its former rank was {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{jalink|Maruyama River|Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{jalink|Kyotango|Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{jalink|Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{jalink|Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{jalink|honden|Q2044994}} is a national {{jalink|Important Cultural Property|Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{jalink|Hakuhō|Q11580612}} 3 ({{jalink|675|Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{jalink|Kokushi Monjo}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{jalink|Koshikoden|Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{jalink|Hokke-ji|Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} 1 ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 7 ({{jalink|1918|Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{jalink|mikoshi|Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{jalink|otabisho|Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{jalink|Eikyō|Q1193059}} 10 ({{jalink|1438|Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{jalink|Kakitsu|Q1193238}} 1 ({{jalink|1441|Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{jalink|Hōtoku|Q1143606}} 1 ({{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}} 6 ({{jalink|1823|Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{jalink|Ukemochi-no-Mikoto}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{jalink|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
=== Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property (national designation) * Honden (with two munafuda)
=== Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'' Vol.19 San'indō 2, Kogakkan University Press, 1984 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Gazette'' Vol.2, Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 edition) * ''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia'', Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7
=== Related items === * {{jalink|Umenomiya Taisha|Q276781}} - enshrines Sakakeko deity seen in ''Kokushi Monjo''; relation to this shrine as brewing deity has been noted regardless of source authenticity. * {{jalink|Tamaki Matsuri Kitaru Sakake Shrine|Q11612891}} - same context.
=== External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine] (Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)

{{Infobox religious building | name = Sakatare Shrine | native_name = 酒垂神社 | image = Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Sakatare Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.655205|134.82546}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Q112299527|lt=Q112299527|WD=Q112299527}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sakatare Shrine'''|酒垂神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tajima Province]].

== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}{{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto}} / {{jalink|Sakamizume-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287}} / former {{jalink|Village Shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|Hokkeiji}}, {{jalink|Toyooka|Q696388}}, {{jalink|Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290}}. See {{jalink|Koshikoden|Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{jalink|Hōtoku|Q1143606}} 1 ({{jalink|1449|Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{jalink|Hōei|Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}} 6 ({{jalink|1823|Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{jalink|Ukemochi-no-Mikoto}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{jalink|Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
=== Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property (national designation) * Honden (with two munafuda)
=== Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'' Vol.19 San'indō 2, Kogakkan University Press, 1984 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Gazette'' Vol.2, Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 edition) * ''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia'', Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7
=== Related items === * {{jalink|Umenomiya Taisha|Q276781}} - enshrines Sakakeko deity seen in ''Kokushi Monjo''; relation to this shrine as brewing deity has been noted regardless of source authenticity. * {{jalink|Tamaki Matsuri Kitaru Sakake Shrine|Q11612891}} - same context.
=== External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine] (Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)

== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill||lt=Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} / {{ill||lt=Sakamizume-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}} / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill||lt=Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Maruyama River|Q11394765|WD=Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{ill|lt=Kyotango|Q513897|WD=Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{ill|lt=Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|WD=Q11673228|lt=Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{ill|lt=Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|WD=Q18336828|lt=Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} 3 ({{ill|lt=675|Q30523|WD=Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{ill||lt=Kokushi Monjo|UNKNOWN}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{ill|lt=Koshikoden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{ill|lt=Hokke-ji|Q3076936|WD=Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{ill|lt=otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{ill|lt=Eikyō|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1438|Q6377|WD=Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{ill|lt=Hōtoku|Q1143606|WD=Q1143606}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{ill|lt=Hōei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1823|Q6988|WD=Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{ill||lt=Ukemochi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{ill||lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
=== Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property (national designation) * Honden (with two munafuda)

=== Related items === * {{ill|lt=Umenomiya Taisha|Q276781|WD=Q276781}} - enshrines Sakakeko deity seen in ''Kokushi Monjo''; relation to this shrine as brewing deity has been noted regardless of source authenticity. * {{ill|lt=Tamaki Matsuri Kitaru Sakake Shrine|Q11612891|WD=Q11612891}} - same context.

[[JA:酒垂神社 (豊岡市)]]
{{wikidata link|Q11644305}}
== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizume-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}} / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Maruyama River|Q11394765|WD=Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{ill|lt=Kyotango|Q513897|WD=Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{ill|lt=Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|WD=Q11673228|lt=Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{ill|lt=Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|WD=Q18336828|lt=Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} 3 ({{ill|lt=675|Q30523|WD=Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|UNKNOWN}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{ill|lt=Koshikoden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{ill|lt=Hokke-ji|Q3076936|WD=Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{ill|lt=otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{ill|lt=Eikyō|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1438|Q6377|WD=Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{ill|lt=Hōtoku|Q1143606|WD=Q1143606}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{ill|lt=Hōei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1823|Q6988|WD=Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukemochi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|UNKNOWN}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
=== Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property (national designation) * Honden (with two munafuda)
=== Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'' Vol.19 San'indō 2, Kogakkan University Press, 1984 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Gazette'' Vol.2, Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 edition) * ''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia'', Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7
=== Related items === * {{ill|lt=Umenomiya Taisha|Q276781|WD=Q276781}} - enshrines Sakakeko deity seen in ''Kokushi Monjo''; relation to this shrine as brewing deity has been noted regardless of source authenticity. * {{ill|lt=Tamaki Matsuri Kitaru Sakake Shrine|Q11612891|WD=Q11612891}} - same context.
=== External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine] (Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)

{{nihongo|'''Sakatare Shrine'''|酒垂神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tajima Province]].

It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{ill|lt=otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{ill|lt=Eikyō|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1438|Q6377|WD=Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{ill|lt=Hōtoku|Q1143606|WD=Q1143606}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{ill|lt=Hōei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1823|Q6988|WD=Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukemochi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
=== Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property (national designation) * Honden (with two munafuda)

== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizume-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}} / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Maruyama River|Q11394765|WD=Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{ill|lt=Kyotango|Q513897|WD=Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{ill|lt=Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|WD=Q11673228|lt=Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{ill|lt=Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|WD=Q18336828|lt=Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} 3 ({{ill|lt=675|Q30523|WD=Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{ill|lt=Koshikoden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{ill|lt=Hokke-ji|Q3076936|WD=Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{ill|lt=otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{ill|lt=Eikyō|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1438|Q6377|WD=Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{ill|lt=Hōtoku|Q1143606|WD=Q1143606}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{ill|lt=Hōei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1823|Q6988|WD=Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukemochi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>

{{Infobox religious building | name = Sakatare Shrine | native_name = 酒垂神社 | image = Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Sakatare Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|35.655205|134.82546}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = {{ill|Q112299527|lt=Q112299527|WD=Q112299527}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Sakatare Shrine'''|酒垂神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tajima Province]].

== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizume-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287}} / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Maruyama River|Q11394765|WD=Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{ill|lt=Kyotango|Q513897|WD=Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{ill|lt=Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|WD=Q11673228|lt=Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{ill|lt=Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|WD=Q18336828|lt=Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} 3 ({{ill|lt=675|Q30523|WD=Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{ill|lt=Koshikoden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{ill|lt=Hokke-ji|Q3076936|WD=Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{ill|lt=otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{ill|lt=Eikyō|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1438|Q6377|WD=Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{ill|lt=Hōtoku|Q1143606|WD=Q1143606}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{ill|lt=Hōei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1823|Q6988|WD=Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukemochi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Maruyama River|Q11394765|WD=Q11394765}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{ill|lt=Kyotango|Q513897|WD=Q513897}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{ill|lt=Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|WD=Q11673228|lt=Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{ill|lt=Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|WD=Q18336828|lt=Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622}}.
=== Shrine name === The name is read "Sakatare." According to shrine tradition it means "slope drooping" based on location, but because its deity is a sake deity it is also called "Sakataru" by association with sake barrels<ref name="report"/><ref>''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture''.</ref>. In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.
=== Enshrined deities === It enshrines '''Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto''' and '''Sakamizume-no-Mikoto'''.
These are said to be ancestral deities of brewers and identical with Sakamizuo/Sakamizume deities enshrined in the Imperial Court's brewing office as guardian deities.
=== Origin === According to shrine tradition, in summer of {{ill|lt=Hakuhō|Q11580612|WD=Q11580612}} 3 ({{ill|lt=675|Q30523|WD=Q30523}}), district official Mononobe no Karakuni no Muraji Kukubi-no-Mikoto, who governed this area, built a sake brewery in sacred rice fields and enshrined three brewing deities (Sakakeko-no-Kami, Ōtoke-ko-no-Kami, and Kotoke-ko-no-Kami) to brew sacred sake and pray for good harvest. But this tradition depends on ''{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Kokushi Monjo|1=UNKNOWN}}'' and its reliability is questioned<ref>''Kokushi Monjo'' is said to have been compiled in 975 by Yōkō Mashika and is treated as doubtful, though some accept old traditions in it (Keiichi Imai, ''Ame no Hiboko'', Sōgeisha, 1966). See {{ill|lt=Koshikoden|Q11410976|WD=Q11410976}}.</ref>, so foundation remains unknown. Because the place name Hokkeiji evokes {{ill|lt=Hokke-ji|Q3076936|WD=Q3076936}} in Nara, considered the head provincial nunnery, there are theories linking this shrine with Tajima's provincial nunnery, provincial office, and district office<ref name="report"/>. It was listed as a small shrine under the Engi system. A shrine relocation ceremony was held in {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}), but whether it was a transfer from another location is unknown.
In {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}), it was ranked village shrine, and on June 15, {{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1918|Q2094|WD=Q2094}}), designated a shinsen-heihaku-ryō kyōshin shrine.
=== Rituals === Current annual festival day is October 15; a {{ill|lt=mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}} with lion dance and others proceeds to the {{ill|lt=otabisho|Q11489117|WD=Q11489117}}. Formerly the festival was on old lunar Sept 10, with a special rite that raised sakaki, invited the divine spirit, and offered sacred sake brewed at the shrine<ref name="report"/>.
=== Shrine buildings === The honden is one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} with kokera roof, protected by a covering structure. Munafuda indicate adze-beginning on Nov 18, {{ill|lt=Eikyō|Q1193059|WD=Q1193059}} 10 ({{ill|lt=1438|Q6377|WD=Q6377}}), pillar-raising on Feb 26, {{ill|lt=Kakitsu|Q1193238|WD=Q1193238}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1441|Q6402|WD=Q6402}}), and relocation rite in Bunan 1 (1444). Ink inscriptions on prayer texts found behind kaerumata indicate continued detailed work in Bunan 5 and {{ill|lt=Hōtoku|Q1143606|WD=Q1143606}} 1 ({{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}), suggesting final completion around Hōtoku 1<ref name="report"/>. Munafuda also show that agents and officials of Hōdō-ji (estate lord of Kamada-shō), shrine priests, village heads, and peasants cooperated in fund-raising; master carpenter Ban Daiyu Ōtomo Hisakiyo and 12 assistant carpenters undertook the work; and relocation rites included Great Prajñā recitation, court music, and kagura offerings. The clear identification of the founding master carpenter is unusual<ref name="jinjacho">Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines, "[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine]" (viewed 2010-04-20).</ref>.
Though small as a honden, it is comparatively large for one-bay shrines and has bold design. A balustraded veranda wraps three sides of the core; rear is simplified with side screens; a lower "hama-yuka" is provided below front stairs. Inside, board doors with bar shafts divide inner/outer sanctums; inner sanctum is one step higher, with front sliding lattice doors. Features follow mid-Muromachi archaic style, with regional Tajima traits in details such as tahei-zuka gable ornaments and eave bracket forms. Unusual are openwork biwa boards at kōhai center featuring a dharma wheel and hexagonal motifs<ref name="ency"/>. Major remodeling around eaves was done in {{ill|lt=Hōei|Q1145262|WD=Q1145262}} 6-8 (1709-1711)<ref name="report"/>, likely when octagonal balustrade members were introduced, showing early modern Tajima regional style<ref name="ency"/>. For these values it was designated national Important Cultural Property on May 14, 1958 (Shōwa 33). Repairs were done in 1913 and dismantling restoration in 1969, when it was restored to its original Muromachi form.
The honden covering structure was newly built during Hōei major remodeling, rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1823|Q6988|WD=Q6988}}), and rebuilt again during Shōwa repairs<ref name="report"/>. The {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} is irimoya-zukuri with tile roof.
=== Subsidiary shrines === There are Inari Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ukemochi-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}), Yamagami Shrine ({{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q386563|lt=Ōyamatsumi-no-Kami|1=UNKNOWN}}), and Hachiman Shrine. Of these, Yamagami Shrine stood in Aza Yamanokami<ref>Aza Yamanokami lot 923-1.</ref> and was moved into the precinct in Taishō 3.
=== Precincts === At both sides of the torii base at the entrance are jar-stones (kame-ishi), said to represent jars for drawing sake<ref name="jinjacho"/>. Two giant sacred cedars, each about 600 years old, once stood west of the torii but were cut in 1984 due to danger of dieback; a "Great Cedar Memorial Stone" now stands there. One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
=== Cultural properties === ; Important Cultural Property (national designation) * Honden (with two munafuda)
=== Footnotes === {{Reflist|2}}
=== References === * ''Shikinaisha Survey Report'' Vol.19 San'indō 2, Kogakkan University Press, 1984 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Shrine Gazette'' Vol.2, Rinsen Shoten, 1984 ISBN 4-653-01102-8 (reprint of 1938 edition) * ''Place Names of Hyogo Prefecture I'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 29), Heibonsha, 1999 ISBN 4-582-49060-3 * ''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia'', Kobe Shimbun Publishing Center, 1983 ISBN 4-87521-100-7
=== Related items === * {{ill|lt=Umenomiya Taisha|Q276781|WD=Q276781}} - enshrines Sakakeko deity seen in ''Kokushi Monjo''; relation to this shrine as brewing deity has been noted regardless of source authenticity. * {{ill|lt=Tamaki Matsuri Kitaru Sakake Shrine|Q11612891|WD=Q11612891}} - same context.
=== External links === *[http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6322039.html Sakataru Shrine] (Hyogo Prefectural Association of Shinto Shrines)

{{nihongo|'''Sakatare Shrine'''|酒垂神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Tajima Province]].

== Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Sakatare Shrine |画像 = [[画像:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka002.jpg|270px]] |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}Hokkeiji, Aza Chōrakuji 725-1 |位置 = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizuo-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakamizume-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha small shrine|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} / former {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} with kokera roof |別名 = Ōkura (Kur a) Daimyōjin |札所 = |例祭 = October 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Hyogo|label=Sakatare Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=9|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka.jpg|thumb|250px|Precincts]] '''Sakatare Shrine''' (さかたれじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hokkeiji (Toyooka)|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|lt=Toyooka|Q696388|WD=Q696388|1=Toyooka, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|lt=Hyogo Prefecture|Q130290|WD=Q130290|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and its former rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}.
It is located at the eastern edge of the flat valley formed by the Kamatani River, a tributary of the {{ill|lt=Maruyama River|Q11394765|WD=Q11394765|1=Maruyama River}}, at the pass entrance toward Kumihama in {{ill|lt=Kyotango|Q513897|WD=Q513897|1=Kyōtango}}. In the precinct, autumn leaves transplanted from {{ill|lt=Takao (Kyoto)|Q11673228|WD=Q11673228|lt=Takao|1=Takao}} in Kyoto and the Kamatani River modeled on Takao's {{ill|lt=Kiyotaki River (Kyoto)|Q18336828|WD=Q18336828|lt=Kiyotaki River|1=Kiyotaki River}} make it a known spot for autumn foliage<ref name="ency">''Hyogo Prefecture Encyclopedia''.</ref><ref name="report">''Shikinaisha Survey Report''.</ref>. The {{ill|lt=honden|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994|1=Honden}} is a national {{ill|lt=Important Cultural Property|Q1188622|WD=Q1188622|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.

In medieval and early modern times it was also called '''Ōkura (Kura) Daimyōjin'''.

One jar-stone is said to have been found at the cedar-cut site<ref name="jinjacho"/>. <gallery> ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone002.jpg|Jar-stone (right) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Stone.jpg|Jar-stone (left) ファイル:Sakataru-Jinjya Toyooka003.jpg|Great cedar memorial stone </gallery>
